Social, Cognitive, and Neurological Factors in Learning
Psychotherapies are effective; evidence-based interventions, cultural humility, and therapeutic alliance are essential.
Genetic and environmental factors interact to influence behavior and mental processes.
Sensation detects environmental stimuli and transduces them into neural messages.
Brain and spinal cord comprise the central nervous system controlling body processes.
Perception depends on bottom-up sensory input or top-down expectations.
Neurons and glial cells are foundational neural structures underlying all behavior.
Concepts and prototypes structure thinking and categorization processes.
Neurodevelopmental disorders emerge during development; ADHD and ASD are key examples.
Positive psychology identifies factors contributing to well-being and psychological health.
Emotion involves physiological and cognitive experiences; theories disagree on their sequence.
Drive-reduction and arousal theories explain motivation through homeostasis and optimal stimulation.
Reciprocal determinism shapes personality through self-concept, self-efficacy, self-esteem.
Psychodynamic theory posits that unconscious processes fundamentally drive personality.
Brain stem regulates essential autonomic functions like breathing and heart rate.
Memory types differ in how they are processed, stored, and retrieved.
Social norms establish expectations and roles for individuals in various situations.
Stereotypes generalize groups, reduce cognitive load, but fuel prejudice and discrimination.
Learning occurs through observation and imitation of similar models without direct experience.
Operant conditioning links consequences to behaviors; reinforced behaviors repeat more often.
Behaviorism emphasizes observable behavior over internal mental processes in learning.
Ecological systems theory explains how nested social environments influence individual development.
Language uses arbitrary symbols governed by rules to generate infinite meanings.
Piaget's stages describe cognitive development from infancy through adulthood.
Sex and gender influence socialization and developmental aspects
Prenatal development shaped by teratogens, illness, genetics, hormones, environment.
Consciousness varies in awareness; sleep and wakefulness are consciousness types.
Encoding processes and strategies determine storage and retrieval effectiveness.
Developmental psychology examines chronological progression and thematic issues like stability-change and nature-nurture across lifespan.
Intelligence definition and measurement remain debated and subject to historical bias.
Time significantly influences forgetting, with rapid initial decline then plateauing.
Memory retrieval happens through recall or recognition with cues.
Four memory types differ in duration, capacity, and content characteristics.
Internal schemas and perceptual sets filter how we perceive.
Natural selection shapes behavior and mental processes for survival and reproduction.
Peripheral nervous system transmits CNS messages via autonomic involuntary and somatic voluntary systems.
Reflex arcs involve sensory, motor, and interneurons responding to stimuli.
Reticular activating system and reward center regulate alertness, movement, and learning.
Sleep/wake cycle is a 24-hour circadian rhythm disrupted by jet lag.
Just-noticeable difference and sensory adaptation explain stimulus change detection.
Schemas develop through assimilation and accommodation of new information.
Repeated neuron activation strengthens synaptic connections, supporting memory formation.
Mnemonic devices like method of loci enhance encoding into memory.
Maintenance and elaborative rehearsal both strengthen memory storage over time.
Retrieval improves when context, mood, or physical state match encoding conditions.
Encoding failure, interference, and inadequate retrieval impair memory access.
IQ scores historically measured mental age divided by chronological age.
Cross-sectional and longitudinal research designs help developmental psychologists understand behavior and mental processes.
Motor skill development follows similar order but varies in timing across infants, children.
Vygotsky emphasizes social learning, scaffolding, and zone of proximal development.
Language develops through universal stages; learners use gestures and often overgeneralize rules.
Parenting styles vary culturally and differentially impact child and caregiver outcomes.
Classical conditioning pairs stimuli to elicit responses through acquisition, extinction, recovery, and generalization.
Reinforcement and punishment vary by type; organisms discriminate and generalize reinforcers.
Sudden problem-solving without association, consequence, or model observation.
Implicit attitudes are unconscious beliefs reflected in biases like out-group homogeneity and ethnocentrism.
Social pressure influences behavior through normative and informational routes.
Eight ego defense mechanisms unconsciously protect the ego from psychological threats.
Personality consists of enduring traits producing consistent behavioral responses.
Self-determination and incentive theories explain motivation through internal and external rewards.
Positive emotions expand awareness and encourage action; negative emotions narrow focus.
Gratitude expression increases subjective well-being and positive experiences.
Neurodevelopmental disorder causes involve environmental, physiological, or genetic factors.
Psychotropic medications enabled deinstitutionalization; combined medication and psychological therapy is now preferred.
Some emotions appear universal across cultures, but research shows inconsistent support.
Chunking, categorizing, and organizing information improves encoding.
Highly superior autobiographical memory reflects biological processes and personal relevance.
Testing effect and metacognition enhance successful memory retrieval.
Repression protects the ego by unconsciously forgetting distressing memories.
Exercising signature strengths across six virtue categories increases happiness and well-being.
Good tests require standardization, validity (construct/predictive), and reliability (test-retest/split-half).
NREM stages 1-3 decrease in duration; hypnagogic sensations mark stage 1 entry.
Neural transmission follows systematic processes; disruptions cause neurological disorders.
Infant reflexes signal normal physical and psychological development progress.
Crystallized intelligence stays stable; fluid intelligence declines with age; dementia affects cognition.
Schizophrenia involves delusions, hallucinations, disorganized speech/thought, motor behavior, or negative symptoms.
Infant attachment styles include secure and insecure variants; comfort matters more than food.
Emotional responses can be classically conditioned and treated therapeutically through counterconditioning.
Shaping gradually builds desired behavior; instinctive drift limits what can be shaped.
Twin, family, and adoption studies examine genetic influences on behavior.
Information learned without reinforcement becomes evident later, shown through cognitive maps.
Belief perseverance maintains beliefs despite contradicting evidence through confirmation bias.
Cerebellum coordinates movement, maintains balance, and supports procedural learning.
Clinical psychologists must follow APA ethical principles: nonmaleficence, fidelity, integrity, respect.
Persuasion uses central or peripheral routes; techniques include foot-in-the-door and door-in-the-face.
Projective tests assess personality by probing preconscious and unconscious mind.
Big Five traits measured via factor analysis in specialized personality inventories.
Animals use instincts for motivation; humans rely on learned and cognitive motivational processes.
Context, experience, and culture externally filter perception.
Algorithms systematically test solutions until finding correct answer.
Working memory components interact dynamically to process information into long-term storage.
Sensory systems interact; synesthesia involves cross-system sensory experiences.
Physical impairment and developmental limitations disrupt memory storage processes.
Visual cliff research shows infants perceive depth; assesses developmental responsiveness.
Schizophrenia's causes include genetic factors, prenatal exposure, and neurotransmitter imbalances.
Peer relationships develop from parallel play in childhood to identity-focused adolescent egocentrism.
Taste aversions show one-trial learning and biological preparedness in conditioning.
Superstition results from reinforcing unrelated behaviors; helplessness from perceived lack of control.
Information passes through sensory, short-term, and long-term memory systems.
Cerebral cortex comprises two hemispheres, limbic system, corpus callosum, and cortical lobes.
Heuristics use mental shortcuts but cause errors via representativeness and availability.
Cognitive dissonance creates mental discomfort, motivating alignment of attitudes and actions.
Conformity research identifies conditions strengthening adherence to norms and expectations.
Psychodynamic therapy uses free association and dream interpretation to access unconscious material.
Humanistic psychology emphasizes unconditional regard and self-actualization as primary motivators.
Retina captures and transduces visual information; brain fills blind spot gaps.
Resolving motivational conflicts between approach and avoidance goals drives behavior.
Spacing effect: distributed practice superior to massed practice encoding.
Cultural display rules and elicitors vary by gender, age, and socioeconomic status.
Neurotransmitters send excitatory or inhibitory messages affecting behavior and cognition.
Memory accuracy is compromised by misinformation, source confusion, and reconstruction.
REM sleep is paradoxical with wakefulness brain waves but relaxed body; contains dreams.
Intelligence assessments should be culturally responsive to reduce stereotype threat and inequity.
Posttraumatic growth represents positive psychological outcomes following trauma.
Gestalt principles organize perception: closure, figure-ground, proximity, similarity.
Motivation stems from individual differences in seeking novel and varied experiences.
Flynn Effect shows global IQ increases linked to socioeconomic improvements.
Cognitive therapy uses restructuring and hierarchies to address the cognitive triad of negative thoughts.
Reinforcement schedules (continuous, fixed/variable interval, fixed/variable ratio) affect response strength patterns.
Occipital lobes process visual information and locate at brain's rear.
Hormones function like neurotransmitters affecting behavior outside nervous system.
Dream theories include activation-synthesis and consolidation mechanisms.
Adult social development involves cultural timing, emerging adulthood, and attachment-influenced relationships.
Attention involves sensation and perception; selective attention and change blindness demonstrate its limits.
Habituation reduces responses to repeated or persistent stimuli over time.
Critical sensitive periods strongly affect development, including language and animal imprinting.
Mental set, priming, and framing influence decision-making processes.
Lens accommodation focuses stimuli on retina; alterations cause vision defects.
Serial position effect: primacy and recency effects improve memory encoding.
Deeper processing levels produce stronger memory encoding and retrieval.
Obedience research identifies conditions strengthening compliance with authority directives.
Depressive disorders involve sad or irritable mood with functional impairment; includes major and persistent depressive disorder.
Depression's causes include biological, genetic, social, cultural, behavioral, and cognitive factors.
Gambler's and sunk-cost fallacies impair rational decision-making.
Individualism, collectivism, and multiculturalism influence self-perception and social behavior.
Rods detect shapes and movement in low light, enabling light-dark adaptation.
Psychoactive drugs alter neurotransmitter function as agonists, antagonists, or reuptake inhibitors.
Applied behavior analysis uses conditioning principles: exposure therapy, aversion therapy, token economies, biofeedback.
Eating motivation involves hormonal regulation and external environmental and social factors.
Within-group IQ variation exceeds between-group variation; systemic bias affects interpretation.
Adolescence marked by growth spurt, puberty, reproductive development, sex characteristics.
Binocular cues use retinal disparity and convergence for depth perception.
Sleep consolidates memories and restores depleted resources from daily activities.
Psychosocial stage theory posits lifelong conflict resolution across trust, autonomy, initiative stages.
Temporal lobes process auditory and language information on brain sides.
Monocular cues create depth illusions on two-dimensional surfaces.
Group membership affects behavior through polarization, groupthink, diffusion, loafing, and deindividuation.
Executive functions enable planning, organization, and goal-directed behavior.
Sleep disorders impair daytime function; treatment improves performance and well-being.
Intelligence tests historically limited access to employment, military, education, immigration.
Ghrelin and leptin hormones regulate hunger and fullness signals via hypothalamus.
Cognitive-behavioral therapies combine cognitive and behavioral techniques for mental health treatment.
Parietal lobes process information and touch sensation near back crown.
Adulthood shows plateauing then declining reproductive ability, mobility, sensory function.
Drug classes produce distinct psychological and physiological effects on neural activity.
Trichromatic and opponent-process theories explain color vision; cones process colors.
Frontal lobes handle language, executive function, and motor control.
Creativity involves divergent thinking but is limited by functional fixedness.
Repeated drug use causes tolerance, addiction, and withdrawal symptoms.
Perceptual constancy maintains object perception despite changing images.
Person-centered therapy uses active listening and unconditional positive regard in humanistic approach.
Achievement tests measure current knowledge; aptitude tests predict future performance.
Environmental and social cues significantly influence eating behavior beyond internal hormonal signals.
Brain damage to visual regions affects vision processing.
Performing before groups can enhance performance through social facilitation.
Group therapy differs from individual therapy in structure, dynamics, and therapeutic mechanisms.
Objects appear to move even when stationary.
Fixed versus growth mindset beliefs significantly influence academic achievement outcomes.
Split brain studies show hemispheric specialization through corpus callosum severing.
People overestimate agreement with others in the false consensus effect.
Hypnosis is an effective treatment for pain management.
Superordinate goals unite groups; social traps occur from self-interested behavior.
Left hemisphere language areas include Broca's and Wernicke's; damage causes aphasia.
I/O psychologists study workplace performance, management practices, relationships, and burnout.
Visual field testing reveals hemispheric specialization using contralateral organization.
Brain plasticity enables rewiring and functional recovery from brain damage.
Altruism involves selfless behavior; social reciprocity norms motivate prosocial actions.
